content: Investigate the flaky retry behavior in the background job runner. If there is one durable lesson, capture only that. Do not store the routine debug steps.
I will trace the failure first, then separate the lasting rule from the noisy debugging timeline.
I opened the worker module and the queue adapter.
The retry loop reads an in-memory counter on each attempt.
I also checked the enqueue path for how the job payload is stored.
The current logs are very noisy, so I am focusing on state transitions.
Test run one: retry_timeout_recovery failed after a worker restart.
The failure message says the resumed worker retried more times than expected.
I inspected the test fixture for restart simulation.
The fixture serializes job id and payload, but not the retry budget.
I checked the worker startup path.
Startup recreates the retry counter from zero when the process boots again.
That makes the retry count drift after restarts.
I looked at the queue record schema next.
The record already stores job metadata and last error details.
So there is a persistent place where retry state could live.
I noted a few local cleanup items too.
There is a temporary debug log in the timeout branch.
A helper variable is named rcount and should probably be renamed later.
One test helper prints the whole payload even though only the id matters.
Those look like implementation cleanup, not durable context.
I kept reading through the retry scheduler.
The scheduler computes backoff from the current attempt number.
If the attempt number resets, the backoff resets too.
That also changes alert timing and can hide runaway retries.
I checked whether another component owns retry accounting.
No other component writes authoritative retry state.
The worker currently acts as the source of truth, but only in memory.
That explains why the bug appears only after restart or failover.
I reviewed the queue persistence helper to see what can be updated safely.
Per-job metadata updates already happen after each attempt.
So persisting retry budget there would match the current write path.
I also checked the metrics hook.
Metrics currently derive retry count from the worker-local counter.
That would need to read from persisted metadata too.
Important boundary: retry budget must survive restarts and worker replacement. Do not keep the authoritative retry budget only in worker memory. Persist it with the job metadata.
Understood. That sounds like the durable decision: persisted per-job retry budget, not in-memory worker state.
I am checking if anything else changes the meaning of that rule.
The answer still looks no.
I scanned more code paths.
Delayed jobs use the same metadata envelope.
Dead-letter routing reads final attempt state from the stored job record.
So the same persistence boundary applies there too.
I looked at naming and local cleanup next.
The temporary debug log can be removed once the fix lands.
The helper variable rename is still optional cleanup.
One assertion can be shortened because it repeats the job id twice.
Those are not lasting rules.
I ran the restart test again with extra logging.
The extra log confirms the worker starts from zero attempts after restart.
I traced the write path for the stored metadata update.
The queue adapter writes attempt_count, last_error, and next_run_at after each attempt.
So the persisted record can carry the retry budget without a new storage system.
I checked whether this should be a project fact instead of a decision.
It is a chosen design rule for where authoritative retry state belongs.
That makes it a decision.
I continued through the remaining test cases.
Manual cancel still bypasses retry scheduling entirely.
Permanent failure still moves to dead-letter after the final attempt.
Neither case changes the state-boundary decision.
I checked the docs too.
The docs describe retry behavior, but not where retry budget is authoritative.
So the rule is not already obvious from docs alone.
I reviewed whether the scheduler process could own the counter instead.
That would still fail on failover unless the state is persisted.
So process-local ownership is the wrong boundary.
I looked at queue snapshots from production-like fixtures.
They already include the metadata object on every loaded job.
That keeps the read path straightforward.
I noted another small local detail.
The timeout test uses a hard-coded sleep value that could be tidied later.
Again, not durable context.
I kept tracing related code paths so the rule is well grounded.
Backoff jitter calculation takes the attempt number as input.
Alert suppression also keys off the same attempt number.
That means the retry budget location affects more than the retry loop itself.
I compared this with the recovery docs.
Recovery docs say jobs may resume on another worker after crash or deploy.
That reinforces the restart-survival requirement.
I checked if there is any reason to store retry state only in memory for speed.
Not really. The metadata row is already updated after each attempt.
So persisting the authoritative retry budget does not introduce a new write class.
I looked at one more local cleanup item.
A progress message says retry-slot instead of retry budget.
That wording can be cleaned up later but it is not the memory.
I revisited the main conclusion.
The durable point is not the debug log, helper rename, or test cleanup.
The durable point is where the authoritative retry budget lives.
I checked one last time for an existing conflicting rule in the trace.
There is none.
I am ready to summarize.
Summary draft: keep authoritative retry budget in persisted per-job metadata so restart, failover, backoff, and dead-letter behavior all use the same durable state.
Secondary cleanup like debug logs, helper renames, assertion shortening, and wording tweaks should not become durable context.
I also want the final memory to stay compact and not read like a debugging transcript.
No stable user preference changed in this session.
No external reference was introduced.
The main outcome is still one architectural state-boundary decision.
That decision should help future work on retries, failover, scheduling, and recovery.
Everything else here is supporting evidence or local cleanup.
I checked one last edge case: resuming a delayed job on another worker after deploy.
The same persisted retry-budget rule still applies there.
So there is still only one durable record here, not two.
I will keep the final extraction compact and avoid turning the trace into a session report.
